topic: A person finds out their surprise party has been cancelled
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox with the dull thud of something unwelcome. "Please be advised that the surprise celebration scheduled for Saturday has been cancelled." I stared at the screen, my jaw tightening. A slow, simmering heat began to rise in my chest. I didn't even bother clicking on the attachment to see the excuse they’d concocted. It was so transparent, so utterly lacking in sincerity. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight, and let out a breath that was more of a scoff than a sigh. The whole elaborate setup, the whispered plans, the carefully curated secrecy—all dissolved into nothingness because of some flimsy administrative oversight. I picked up my phone, not to call anyone, but just to feel the solid weight of it in my hand, a tangible contrast to the airy emptiness of the cancellation notice.

[story 2]

"Cancelled." The word echoed in my head, sharp and brittle. I reread the message three times, my eyes scanning the text as if searching for some hidden flaw, some deliberate misdirection. A tight knot formed just beneath my sternum, a sensation that made my shoulders involuntarily hunch up toward my ears. I didn't move immediately. Instead, I slowly closed the laptop lid, the click sounding unnaturally loud in the quiet apartment. When I finally stood up, the movement was stiff, deliberate. I walked over to the window, looking out at the indifferent cityscape. There was no anger bubbling up, just a profound sense of being utterly unimpressed. They thought they could just wipe the slate clean with a bureaucratic note. Pathetic.

[story 3]

I picked up the notification, my thumb hovering over the 'delete' icon before I decided against it. The sheer audacity of it—the thought that someone could dismantle such a carefully orchestrated event with a single, impersonal notification—made my lips curl into a thin, tight line. I let the paper flutter from my fingers onto the desk. My gaze swept across the room, lingering on the decorations that were now just discarded remnants of a fantasy. A low, humorless chuckle escaped me, dry and rasping. I didn't waste time drafting a response. There was no need to beg for an explanation, no desire to probe for the real reason behind this sudden, disappointing deflation. I simply tucked the paper into a drawer, the action precise and final.
